description: Backlog of features. The leader dispatches one pending feature at a time whose depends_on are all done; the implementer builds it; the reviewer approves before it flips to done.

model_provider.name: OpenRouter
model_provider.notes: OpenAI-compatible gateway; one API key proxies both DeepSeek tiers. All model access goes through it — no direct DeepSeek or Anthropic SDK.

sdk.name: OpenAI Agents SDK
sdk.notes: Orchestration primitive (agent + tool loop), pointed at OpenRouter's base_url. The deterministic controller still owns ALL routing; the SDK's tool_input_guardrail supplies the code-side pre-tool-call deny gate (nested-agent + .env blocks).

researcher.why: Only heavy multi-turn tool user (web + file); cheap-tier tool-call accuracy collapses on multi-turn orchestration (BFCL V4), and a fabricated or mis-formatted citation is a hard-to-detect, hard-to-reverse leak into the thesis.

writer.why: Produces the actual deliverable and is the only agent that repairs its own work across many conflicting-feedback revision turns; gates detect but never fix, so draft quality dominates cost at this document volume. Assigned the qwen_plus tier (qwen/qwen3.7-plus) — deliberately a different model family from the deepseek Pro Judge, so the terminal gate does not share the Writer's blind spots. Re-verify the qwen3.7-plus slug on OpenRouter at implementation time.

reviewer.why: Read-only, non-terminal, 2-pass soft gate backstopped by the Pro Judge; worst case is one wasted Writer revision — the single bounded/backstopped place to spend the cheap tier.

judge.why: Terminal pass/fail gate with no downstream check (a false APPROVE ships); mirrors the original's deliberate opus-for-the-gate choice. Quote machine-verification guards the evidence, not the judgment, so the reasoning itself must be the strongest tier.

notes: Per-agent tiers were originally decided via a 4-lens judge panel that landed on pro/pro/flash/pro (researcher/writer/reviewer/judge); the Writer was subsequently moved from the pro tier to qwen_plus (qwen/qwen3.7-plus) — see writer.why.

Judge and Writer are now different model families (Judge = deepseek pro, Writer = qwen_plus), so the terminal verifier no longer shares the generator's blind spots — this removes the self-agreement risk that existed when both were deepseek pro. Separately, as of the create-mode redesign in architecture.md §6.2, create mode has no human final-draft checkpoint left as a backstop (it runs unattended end-to-end, gated only by mode selection at the CLI), so the machine-verified verbatim-quote requirement and the fixed rubric are the only line of defense against a false APPROVE.

Reviewer is the closest call and the pipeline's thin spot — escalate flash -> pro if it starts rubber-stamping (Judge rejects drafts the Reviewer approved in >~30% of runs, thin/contradictory feedback, or {approved, feedback} parse failures).

Structured output for Reviewer/Judge uses prompting + code-side validation, NOT native tool-call JSON (DeepSeek native function-calling accuracy collapses ~50pts vs prompting on BFCL V4). Keep the controller's verbatim-quote machine-check.
Model slugs are config-driven; the deepseek-v4-* names supersede the retired deepseek-chat / deepseek-reasoner aliases — re-verify the current slugs on OpenRouter at implementation time.
At most one feature may be 'in_progress' at a time.
A feature may only be 'in_progress' or 'done' if all its depends_on are 'done'.
Only the implementer flips a feature to 'done', and only after reviewer approval.
Each feature lists the files it may touch in 'paths' and its 'acceptance' criteria.

features.name: project_skeleton
acceptance: uv-managed package skeleton per .claude/rules/architecture.md §3 (config.py + package layers + tests).
config.py is the only module reading os.environ; exposes load_config().
uv run pytest tests -q -m 'not integration' is green with at least one smoke test.
uv run ruff check . and ruff format --check . are clean.
bash init.sh finishes with [OK] Environment ready.

features.name: openrouter_client
acceptance: The OpenAI Agents SDK is configured against OpenRouter's OpenAI-compatible base_url (stack.model_provider.base_url); the API key is read only in config.py from env, with no default, and fails loudly if missing.
The configured model slugs in stack.models (pro / flash / qwen_plus) are config constants, never inlined at call sites; tier selection resolves any configured tier by name.
Per-call token usage is captured for cost accounting, and a per-stage maxTurns cap is enforceable.
Unit test mocks the HTTP/client layer (no real network call) and asserts the client targets the OpenRouter base_url and selects the requested tier.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: agent_definitions
acceptance: The four agents (researcher, writer, reviewer, judge) are defined with the per-agent model tier from stack.agent_models (researcher=pro, writer=qwen_plus, reviewer=flash, judge=pro) and least-privilege tool scoping (only the researcher gets web tools).

A code-side pre-tool-call guardrail (OpenAI Agents SDK tool_input_guardrail, or an equivalent dispatcher wrapper) denies nested agent/task spawning and any tool access to .env — enforced in code, not by prompt.
Agent system prompts live in prompts/ as files, not inline strings.

Reviewer and Judge produce their structured output via prompting + code-side jsonschema validation (not native tool-call JSON); the Judge's per-criterion verbatim quotes remain machine-verifiable against the draft.
Unit tests assert each agent's model slug and tool scope, and that the guardrail denies a .env read and a nested-spawn attempt.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: agent_result_logging
acceptance: Every run_agent() call (core/controller.py) emits exactly one structured log line per conventions.md §5 (logger.info(json.dumps(...)), never print()) containing at minimum: agent name, pipeline stage, model tier/slug, run_id, duration_ms, prompt/completion token usage, derived cost, and success/failure outcome.
A run_id is generated once per pipeline invocation (create_mode/review_mode) and threaded through every run_agent() call in that run, so all four agents' log lines from one run share the same run_id.

When config.py's trace_dir is set (env-overridable, default data/output/traces/), each run_agent() call additionally appends one full JSON record (including the untruncated prompt and output, not just the summary) to <trace_dir>/<run_id>.jsonl — a complete, local-only, replayable per-run transcript across all four agents.

No new third-party dependency is added for this (no LangChain, no LangSmith): LangChain's orchestration model would conflict with the deterministic controller owning all routing (architecture.md §2, §4), and LangSmith's default tracing uploads full prompts/completions — i.e. thesis draft content and source excerpts — to a third-party service and requires a second secret, which contradicts config.py's single-secret invariant (architecture.md §9). Logging stays stdlib logging + local files.

Unit test mocks the model client and asserts: the structured log line's fields are present with correct types and a shared run_id across a multi-agent run; when trace_dir is set, the JSONL file receives exactly one appended line per call with the expected keys.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: schemas
acceptance: DocSpec (Pydantic) carries the architecture §8 fields: title, docType ('thesis-chapter'), format ('docx'|'pptx'), language, chapter{number,title}, audience, targetWords, citationStyle, requirements[], notes — with enums/types enforced.

Review = {approved: bool, feedback: str}; Verdict = {approved: bool, perCriterionScores: CriterionScore[], reasons: str[]}; CriterionScore = {criterionId: str, score: int in 0..5, quotedJustification: str, comment: str}.
Each model exposes its JSON schema (e.g. model_json_schema()); models carry no behavior beyond validation (architecture §4).

Unit tests assert a valid DocSpec and a valid Verdict parse to the expected field values, AND that invalid input (bad format enum, out-of-range score, missing required field) raises pydantic ValidationError.
pydantic is added via uv add with an exact pin (recorded in pyproject.toml + uv.lock).
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: rubric
acceptance: The fixed six-criterion rubric is compiled-in per architecture §7: grounding (pass >=4), references (>=4), scope (>=3), structure (>=3), argument (>=3), style (>=3) — each scored 0..5 with its passing threshold.
rubric_to_text() returns text listing every criterion id, description, and threshold, suitable for embedding in the Judge prompt.
A helper exposes the criterion ids and their thresholds (for parity checks by verify); the passing rule is documented: approved iff every criterion meets its threshold.
Unit tests assert the exact set of six criterion ids and their thresholds, and that rubric_to_text() mentions each criterion.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: verify
acceptance: verify_verdict_quotes(verdict, draft) normalizes text and checks (architecture §6.4): (a) every rubric criterion is scored — a missing or unknown criterionId voids the verdict; (b) each quotedJustification meets a minimum length; (c) each quote occurs verbatim (after normalization) in the draft.
Any failure voids the verdict — forces approved=false with the reason appended — in code, not by debate.

approved is recomputed in code as all(score >= threshold) from the per-criterion scores against the rubric (architecture §7 hardening); a verdict counts as an approval only if it passes BOTH the recomputed thresholds AND the quote check.
The function returns a typed result (the adjusted Verdict / a verification result carrying approved + reasons), never unvalidated free text.

Unit tests assert: a verdict whose quotes are all present and all scores meet threshold stays approved; a fabricated/absent quote voids it (approved false, reason appended); a missing or unknown criterion voids it; a single sub-threshold score flips approved to false via the recompute.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: formats
acceptance: render_to_format(markdown, spec, output_dir, name_hint=None) in adapters/formats/__init__.py slugifies a filename and routes to a renderer by spec.format (architecture §8); output_dir comes from the caller (config), not hard-coded.

prose.py renders the Writer's Markdown subset (#/##/### headings, paragraphs, -/* bullets, 1. numbered items, **bold**/*italic*) to .docx via python-docx and returns the written file path under output_dir.
slides.py (.pptx) is a stub that raises NotImplementedError until implemented.
python-docx is added via uv add with an exact pin (pyproject.toml + uv.lock).

Unit tests (tmp_path as output_dir, no filesystem mocking) assert: a docx spec produces a .docx file whose content reflects the input Markdown (headings/paragraphs present); a pptx spec raises NotImplementedError; slugify produces the expected safe filename.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: controller
acceptance: create_mode(spec, ...) drives Researcher -> Writer(outline) -> Writer(draft) -> Reviewer<->Writer (capped at MAX_REVIEW_PASSES) -> Judge<->Writer (capped at MAX_JUDGE_RETRIES) -> render_to_format, generating one run_id via new_run_id() and threading it through every run_agent() call. It runs UNATTENDED — no human checkpoints (architecture §6.2).

Loop caps are integer counters in code (architecture §6.1); a final Judge rejection after MAX_JUDGE_RETRIES neither loops forever nor prompts a human — the run completes and the controller records the unapproved verdict alongside the rendered output.

The controller never routes on unvalidated free text: Reviewer/Judge outputs are parsed and validated against their Pydantic schema (retried on failure), and the Judge verdict must pass verify_verdict_quotes() plus the recomputed all(score>=threshold) gate before it can count as an approval (architecture §6.3/§6.4/§7).
review_mode(document, ...) runs Reviewer -> Judge (quotes verified) -> assembles a Markdown review report -> render_to_format; no Writer and no revision loop (architecture §2.2).

Unit tests (all agents + model client mocked, no network) assert: create_mode honors MAX_REVIEW_PASSES and MAX_JUDGE_RETRIES (counters not exceeded); a shared run_id across the run; a Judge rejection after the cap still completes and records the unapproved verdict; a fabricated Judge quote is rejected by the code-side verify; review_mode produces a report without invoking a Writer.
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.

features.name: cli
acceptance: cli.py parses --spec <DocSpec.json>, --mode create|review, and --input <file> (for review); validates the DocSpec at the boundary before any model call; loads .env; dispatches to create_mode/review_mode; prints results. Mode is asked interactively if omitted.
Mode selection is the pipeline's ONLY human interaction (architecture §6.2); the entrypoint holds no routing logic beyond mode selection and never imports upward past core/.
.env is loaded here (python-dotenv exact-pinned via uv, or a stdlib parser — not in config.py); OPENROUTER_API_KEY is still resolved via config.py with no default / fail-loud.
python -m thesis_agents runs the CLI (a __main__.py entrypoint).

Unit tests (controller + model mocked, no network, real .env not consulted) assert: a valid --spec/--mode dispatches to the correct mode; an invalid DocSpec is rejected at the boundary with a clear error and no model call is made; a missing --mode triggers the interactive prompt path (stdin mocked).
uv run ruff check . clean; bash init.sh green.
